properties.field_incident_overview:
Evacuations: Curry County Sheriff John Ward has removed all evacation levels for the Moon Complex. 

The Emergency Area and Trail closure for the Moon Complex that went into effect November 14th of 2025, has been rescinded. Effective, February 24th, 2026, this closure was replaced with Emergency Trail Closure 06-10-28-26-04, which re-opens many of the camping areas along the lower Rogue River Corridor. While much of the fire-impacted area has been reopened, recreationists will continue to see effects from the fire and ongoing repair work in the area. 

For closure information, visit: https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/rogue-siskiyou/alerts/rogue-river-national-recreation-trail-emergency-closure 

The remaining Rogue River Trail Closure begins at the western trailhead of the Rogue River Trail (Trail #1160) and follows the trail to the east for approximately 8 miles to Jackson Creek. The trail from Jackson Creek to Clay Hill Creek will be opened by May 1, 2026. The Forest will be assessing the segment from Clay Hill Creek to Foster Bar in the spring of 2026. 

On September 3, a lightning event on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest caused a number of fires in the vicinity of the Rogue River about 11 miles northeast of Agness, Oregon. Six of these made up the initial Moon Complex: the Backbone, Brushy, Pinnacle, Tate, Paradise, and Stair fires. 

The Moon Complex is now in 100% containment status. 

Fire Restrictions: Tuesday, October 16, 2025, The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest decreased the Fire Danger Rating to LOW across all five Ranger Districts and removed Public Use Restrictions forest-wide as well as on the Wild & Scenic section of the Rogue River. To learn more, visit the forest website: https://www.fs.usda.gov/alerts/rogue-siskiyou/alerts-notices

Fire Repair: Repair of fire line and roadways is the primary focus of resources engaged on the Moon Complex
properties.title: Moon Complex

properties.field_incident_overview: The Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire in the Upland Island Wilderness Area started on Tuesday, February 24, 2026. The fire is located approximately 5.9 miles from Zavalla, TX in the Angelina National Forest.
properties.title: Cypress Creek Wilderness Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
Road 203 Fire has been declared controlled. 

The Road 203 Fire was detected at 4:00 pm Thursday, March 12, 2026 on the Bessey Ranger District of the Nebraska National Forest. Ground crews initially responded and received assistance from air resources conducting retardant and water drops. Rocky Mountain Complex IMT 3 assumed command of the fire on Saturday, March 14, 2026.

The Road 203 Fire is burning in heavy dead and down timber and grass. The terrain is steep and remote, making it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. A Nebraska National Forest closure order is in effect.
properties.title: Road 203 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Qury Fire started on March 12, 2026 in Custer, SD. The Qury and Deer Fires were initially individual fires but have combined to one fire. There are evacuation orders in place and roads are closed. For up to date information about evacuations, where to take animals, and road closures, refer to Custer County Emergency Management https://www.facebook.com/CusterCountySDEM/
properties.title: Qury Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
This page will no longer be updated after 6 p.m. on Saturday, March 28, 2026. Please contact the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency at 402-326-3179 with questions regarding the Morrill Fire. 

During the late afternoon of Thursday, March 12, 2026, a wildfire started in Morrill County, Nebraska. The fire was rapidly pushed to the east/southeast by extremely high winds. Despite the heroic efforts of firefighters and community members, the fire extended into Garden, Arthur and Keith Counties. In less than 12 hours, the fire traveled approximately 70 miles. Winds shifted to the northeast early Friday morning, March 13 and the fire is close to the western edge of Grant County. Air tankers arrived on scene early Friday morning. Local law enforcement agencies issued evacuation orders in their jurisdictions and evacuation shelters were established. Agricultural irrigation systems were activated in some areas to try to slow the fire’s spread and protect resources and structures. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires March 14. Rocky Mountain CIMT 1 managed the Ashby, Minor, Cottonwood and Morrill Fires under the direction of the State of Nebraska. The State Emergency Operations Center at the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency remains fully operational to coordinate with Rocky Mountain CIMT 1.

On Saturday, March 28 at 6 p.m. local time, Rocky Mountain CIMT 1 will transfer management responsibility of all four fires in a formal process called transfer of command. Command of the Morrill Fire will be transferred to local fire departments. Command of the Ashby Fire, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and the initial-attack response will be transferred to Rocky Mountain CIMT 2.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Morrill Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started in the southeast corner of Lincoln County during late afternoon on Thursday, March 12 and entered Dawson County later that night. Winds from the west were very high and quickly spread the fire.  Numerous evacuations took place as the fire rapidly spread to the south/southeast. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. Firefighters and community members heroically fought the fire since it started.  

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires on March 14.  At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires. Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

Resources will remain available to provide statewide initial attack response. As with its predecessor, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 will manage all fires under the direction of the State of Nebraska.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Cottonwood Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: This fire was reported on March 18, 2026 at 1:21pm 2 miles southeast of Rockerville, South Dakota.
properties.title: Coyote Flats Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started after midnight on Thursday, March 26 approximately 2 miles north of Ashby in Grant County. Due to high winds, it quickly ran 17 miles southward and burned into Morrill County and the area already burned by the Morrill Fire. An evacuation of Ashby took place as structures were threatened. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) mobilized initial-attack resources from the nearby Morrill and Cottonwood Fires. They actively engaged the fire and assisted local volunteer fire departments and the Nebraska State Fire Marshal’s Wildland Incident Response and Assistance Team. At Nebraska Governor Jim Pillen’s request, Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) 1 assisted with both the Ashby and nearby Minor Fire. 

The incident has additional initial-attack groups staged and ready to immediately respond to requests from local or state officials to assist with the initial suppression of any other new fires that may start. 

 At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and provided statewide initial attack response.  Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Ashby Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started after midnight on Thursday, March 26 approximately 5 miles south of the community of Hyannis in Grant County. Due to high winds, the Minor Fire moved approximately 13 miles southward and nearly entered Arthur County. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) mobilized initial-attack resources from the nearby Morrill and Cottonwood Fires. They actively engaged the fire and assisted local volunteer fire departments and the Nebraska State Fire Marshal’s Wildland Incident Response and Assistance Team. At Nebraska Governor Jim Pillen’s request, Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) 1 assisted with both the Ashby and nearby Minor Fire. 

The incident has additional initial-attack groups staged and ready to immediately respond to requests from local or state officials to assist with the initial suppression of any other new fires that may start. The State Emergency Operations Center (SEOC) at the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency (NEMA) has been and remains fully operational to coordinate with Rocky Mountain CIMT 2.

 At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and provided statewide initial attack response. Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time.  Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

Please visit this link for additional information sources.
properties.title: Minor Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Sago Fire is a full suppression incident. It is currently burning in grass as well as ponderosa pine, piñon, and juniper trees. Crews are actively working to improve and keep the fire within established retardant lines, handlines, and dozer lines. Repair work began on Tuesday, March 31, 2026. Command of the Sago Fire on the Mescalero Reservation was transferred to the Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Incident Management Team Friday at 5 p.m.
properties.title: Sago Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The 79 Fire was declared 100% contained at 5,408 acres on Tuesday afternoon 4.14.26.  State and local resources will continue checking on the fire for several days.

==

The 79 Fire was declared 4.11.26, three miles north of Buffalo Gap, South Dakota. There are state and federal resources supporting the suppression effort.

properties.field_incident_overview:
The East Side Fire ignited on April 20, 2026. 

 All evacuation orders and warnings for the East Side Fire were lifted Wednesday, April 22.
properties.title: East Side Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Gun Range Fire is located on the Osceola National Forest, one mile northeast of Watertown, Florida. The current size is 129 acres with 85% containment. The fire was detected on April 19th. The cause is undetermined.

The wildfire remains within established control lines with minimal fire behavior expected. Firefighters continue to focus on hot spot detection, removing hazard trees and structure protection. 

The Lewis D. Whitaker Osceola Shooting Range is closed until further notice per the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission. 

For current smoke information, visit https://airnow.org

Closures and Restrictions: 

  • Columbia and Baker Counties are under a temporary burn ban. 
  • Open burning of yard debris is prohibited year-round per county ordinance. 

Regarding drones: Please remember, if you fly we can’t!
properties.title: Gun Range

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Sand Drain Fire is located 18 miles northeast of Olustee, Florida on the Osceola National Forest. This human-caused wildfire started April 12th. The size remains at 56 acres and is 100 percent contained as of May 6th, 2026. The fire is currently being managed by U.S. Forest Service - National Forests in Florida personnel.

The Fire remains within established containment lines. Firefighters continue to focus on hot spot detection and hazard tree removal. Structure protection and initial attack resources remain in place. 

For current smoke information, visit airnow.org

Closures and Restrictions: 

  • Columbia and Baker Counties are under a temporary burn ban. 
  • Open burning of yard debris is prohibited year-round per county ordinance. 
  • The Lewis D. Whitaker Osceola Shooting Range is closed until further notice per the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission 

Regarding drones: Please remember, if you fly we can’t!
properties.title: Sand Drain

properties.field_incident_overview:
Reported: 4/22/26 at 6:36 pm

Location: Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest

Jurisdiction: Federal

Andrews Fire Afternoon Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire has been declared 100% contained. Upon more accurating mapping it was determined that the fire is 570 acres. 

Crews will continue to patrol the fire's permieter and within it, doing mop-up and montioring over the next several days. 

The Red Flag Warning is still in effect until 8:00 PM CDT. 

Andrews Fire Morning Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire was reported on April 22, 2026 at approximately 6:36 PM on the Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest. The current estimated burn area is 2500 acres and is being managed by Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands wildland fire crews. The fire has been burning in the north central area of the McKelvie National Forest in grass fuels.

Crews worked through the night with successful suppression efforts and are currently seeing minimal activity. Current strategy for today and the following days will be patrolling, mop-up, and monitoring the burned and active fire areas. Firefighters doing mop-up is a critical part of wildland fire efforts, firefighters extinguish hot spots to prevent reignition.

There are multiple active new wildfires adjacent to the McKelvie National Forest that US Forest Service wildland fire crews, along with local, state, and federal agencies are assisting with in the area.

There is currently a Red Flag Warning in effect through 8:00 PM today, April 23, 2026. Strong winds are forecast to continue throughout today.

Stage 1 Fire Prohibitions remain in place for Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands units.
properties.title: Andrews Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The lightning caused Hummingbird Fire is burning in the Gila Wilderness through dense patches of brush, dead and down fuels, and standing dead trees (snags) left behind from Whitewater-Baldy fire in 2012. After an aggressive initial attack by ground resources, 2 large airtankers and a Type 1 helicopter, the fire continued to grow. The Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Team assumed command of the incident on April 23, 2026. Command was transferred to a type 5 organization on May 20th.
properties.title: Hummingbird Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Hazen Fire transitioned to a Type 4 Organization on Friday, May 8 at 7am. Future updates will be made to this incident as notable activity occurs.

 

History:

The Hazen Fire was detected on Saturday, May 2 at approximately 2:50 pm. The fire is burning in salt cedar, grass and brush approximately 1 mile southwest of Buckeye, AZ. The fire is in the response area for the City of Buckeye Fire Department and AFMA. They provided initial response along with other departments to include Goodyear Fire Department, Surprise Fire-Medical Department and others.  

Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) assumed command of the fire the evening of May 2 at the request of City of Buckeye Fire Department and Arizona Fire & Medical Authority (AFMA). The incident was under the command of a DFFM Type 3 Incident Management Team. Official updates will be provided on social media accounts for DFFM. Updates are provided as they occur and are verified for accuracy.
properties.title: Hazen

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Mapes fire started on the afternoon of May 4th during red flag fire weather conditions and high winds.  

The Mapes Fire is considered 100% contained, meaning that a secure, cold, perimeter is now in place around the entire fire.  

The fire was classified as OUT on May 19, 2026.

No further information is available.
properties.title: Mapes

properties.field_incident_overview: The Six Mile Fire is a wildfire burning in steep terrain within the Magdalena Mountains on the Magdalena Ranger District of the Cibola National Forest & National Grasslands near Socorro, New Mexico. No evacuations are currently in effect,
properties.field_title_and_unit: NMCIF Six Mile Fire Magdalena Ranger District Cibola National Forest Grasslands
properties.title: Six Mile Fire - Magdalena Ranger District - Cibola National Forest & Grasslands

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Jones Fire is 100% contained at 84 acres. All resources have been released and the incident was turned back over to the Wickenburg Fire Department for any further monitoring. All objectives have been met, including fence repair and dozer line rehabilitation. There is no heat remaining along the fire's edge and no visible smokes showing. 

On Friday, crews finished all chainsaw operations and continued line improvement and mop up along the southwest side of the fire. Today's work also included repairing dozer line within the river bottom to clean it up and restore the area to its natural state. 

Starting today, Stage 1 fire restrictions are in place within State Trust lands in Gila, Maricopa, and Pinal Counties. For fire restriction information: WildlandFire.az.gov. 

The US 60 is fully open. Drivers should use caution through the area as engines continue to work alongside the road. 

 

The Jones Fire was reported on May 11 burning approximately 1 mile southeast of Wickenburg, AZ.  Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) is in command of the fire. The fire is burning in the Hassayampa River bottom in salt cedar, cottonwood, grass and brush.

Forward progression was stopped at 6:35pm on May 11.  The fire is under full suppression.

Evacuation Shelter: Red Cross Arizona has opened a shelter for evacuees at Wickenburg High School Gym located 1090 S. Vulture Mine Road, Wickenburg, AZ 85390

Traffic Information: visit az511.com
properties.title: Jones

properties.field_incident_overview:
Ash Pole Fire was discovered 5.13.2026, at 10:36pm.

5.17.2026 Update:

Today, Ash Pole Fire was more accurately mapped at 1028 acres and 90% contained. All future updates will eb available here or on Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands Facebook page.
properties.title: Ash Pole Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
On May 14, 2026, At approximately 4:30 pm, a storm cell came over the area of Randall County creating dry lighting which started a series of wildfires throughout the area. At 4:30 pm Texas A&M Forest Service was requested by Randall County Fire Department for a fire off of FM 1705 and Buffalo Lake Rd. The fire has jumped FM 1714 moving north. Multiple structures are threatened. Texas A&M Forest Service is working in unified command with Canyon Fire Department. Fire engines are currently doing structure protection and heavy equipment is engaging on both flanks. Aviation resources are also being utilized. Forward progression has stopped. 

Update - May 17, 12:00 p.m. - There are no visible smokes on the fire at this time. The Temporary Flight Restriction has been removed. Crews are engaged in mopping-up and patrolling.

Update - May 18, 2026, 8:00 a.m. - Firefighters will continue to patrol the fire's edge and check for any problems. Palo Duro Canyon State Park is opened for visitors to drive through, but all hiking trails are still closed.

Final Update - May 19, 2026 - 6:00 p.m. - The fire is 100% contained and all state resources are off of the fire.
properties.title: Hunggate Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 5:42 pm on Thursday, May 14th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire approximately 11 miles south of Masterson in Potter County, Texas. Texas A&M Forest Service crews are working in unified command with local resources. Local resources are engaged in structure protection. 

As of 5:15 AM on Friday, May 15th, forward progression has been stopped. Crews will continue constructing containment line. 

May 15, 2026 at 3:30 PM: The fire is 100% contained, there will be no further updates. State resources have been released from the fire.

As of 1:25 p.m. on May 16, 2026, the Roman Fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. There will be no additional updates for this fire.

May 15, 2026 at 4:30 PM: The fire is 100% contained, there will be no further updates. State resources have been released from the fire.

May 15, 2026 at 9:34 PM: The fire is 100% contained, there will be no further updates. State resources have been released from the fire.

At 10:43 am on Friday May 15th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire 18 miles West of Childress, Texas. The Chainey Mountain Fire is 3 miles west of FM 94 in Hall County and burning in juniper mixed vegetation. In coordination with local fire departments, Texas A&M Forest Service crews are responded with additional firefighters, heavy equipment, and aviation resources. Crews are constructing containment lines around the perimeter of the fire with existing roads and where necessary.

Update - May 16 - 2:45 p.m. Dozers are continuing to make progress around the left flank of the fire. Few smokes within the containment line remain. 

As of 6:50 p.m. on May 16, 2026, the fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. This is the last planned update for this fire.

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 4:52 PM on Friday, May 15th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire approximately 7 miles south of Canyon, TX in Randall County. Texas A&M Forest Service crews are working in unified command with local resources. Extreme fire behavior due to weather conditions being observed.

May 15th - 7:45 PM - The fire has been turned back to locals for the evening and will be checked in the morning.

As of 12:30 p.m. May 16, 2026, the Western Fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. This is the final update for the fire.
properties.title: Western Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 4:42 PM on Friday, May 15th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire approximately 8 miles west of Rochester, Texas in Haskell County. Texas A&M Forest Service crews are working in unified command with local resources. Moderate to high fire behavior due to weather conditions being observed. 

May 15, 2026 at 10:00 PM: The fire is 100% contained, there will be no further updates. State resources have been released from the fire.
properties.title: Mustache Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 4:57 pm on Friday May 15th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire 9 miles Northeast of Turkry, Texas west of FM Road 657. In coordination with local fire departments, Texas A&M Forest Service resources are responded with additional firefighters, heavy equipment, and aviation resources. Crews are working in unified command with local resources. Moderate to high fire behavior in difficult terrain was observed. 

As of Saturday morning, the fire activity was significantly better, but smokes remain visible. Fire engines will engage in a mapping mission today, and will scout the head of the fire. Dozers will work the head of the fire if conditions allow. 

Update - May 16, 6:45 p.m. - Earlier today, the fire was established in two different draws. The left finger was fully lined and the right finger had resources able to access on foot. There were flare ups earlier in the day on the heel and there was a plan to conduct strategic firing operations to clean up insecure lines. At 5:00 p.m., a burnout operation was conducted on the heel of the fire. This operation went well and resources are patrolling the fire. 

As of 9:00 p.m. on May 16, the fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. This is the final update for the Ox Bow Fire.
properties.title: Ox Bow Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Stewart Trail Fire was detected during the afternoon hours on Friday, May 15, 2026, near Lake County Highway 3 and U.S. Highway 61 in Lake County Minnesota, 3 miles north of Two Harbors.  

The fire is now 100% contained and management of the fire has transitioned to transitioned to a type 4 incident commander with the local Minnesota Department of Natural Resources unit on May 19, 2026.
properties.title: Stewart Trail

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 9:30 PM on Friday, May 15th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire approximately 5 miles east of Northfield, Texas in Motley County. Texas A&M Forest Service crews are working in unified command with local resources. Moderate to high fire behavior due to weather conditions being observed. 

May 17, 2026 Update (2:00 p.m.) - Firefighters conducted a successful burnout operation at the head of the fire today. This operation was conducted in an area of concern where unburned fuel was threatening the containment line. Firefighters are conducting mop-up on this area as well as mop-up and patrol of the fire perimeter. 

As of 10:20 p.m. on May 17, the Comanche Fire is 100% contained. No state resources remain on the fire. This is the final report for the fire.
properties.title: Comanche Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
Current Situation – Evening of May 22, 2026

It was another productive day on the fire today with additional containment lines being established. There was no growth on the fire’s perimeter today and the size of the fire remains at 18,379 acres. The fire is now 72% contained.

When the fire reaches this stage of the incident, firefighters use a multifaceted approach to finding remaining heat sources. This includes everything from using modern technology such as unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) to satellites with infrared technology, along with firefighters on the ground cold trailing where the fire once burned.

Cold trailing is a slow, methodical approach where firefighters literally walk a fire’s edge, looking for any signs of heat. With advances in firefighting techniques, this may at first be done using a handheld infrared sensing tool, but it is also done with feeling for heat on the ground with the back on the hand, sensing for where hidden heat may be underground in roots, not readily visible on the surface. 

Today, fire crews focused on the area south of Soledad Peak and Radar Peak on the west side of the fire. Firefighters on the ground in coordination with the UAS module, worked to find any remaining heat below the peak. They also patrolled the contained line along the South Point Spur to ensure that containment line was still holding. 

On the east side of the fire, the crews assigned in that area worked along the entire Telephone Road and into the Water Canyon Campground to increase containment line in that area. 

Tomorrow, firefighters will continue to track and seek out any remaining heat sources below Soledad Peak and Radar Peak. They will also be working with resources advisors in the Main Ranch Complex to address any suppression repair and returning of supplies from the suppression efforts. 

Background:

On Friday, May 15, 2026, an aircraft flying over Santa Rosa Island in Channel Islands National Park reported a wildfire. National Park Service (NPS) staff already on the island confirmed it later that morning. The Santa Rosa Island Fire is located on the southeastern end of the island in remote and rugged terrain. This is a full-suppression human-caused wildfire and is under investigation.

Closures:

Santa Rosa Island is closed to all day and overnight use through at least June 6, 2026, due to the wildfire. All Water Canyon Campground reservations have been cancelled through this date and visitors have been notified by email. Campground reservation holders with bookings through August 14, 2026, have also been informed that the wildfire may affect their stay. If additional cancellations are needed, they will receive follow-up emails.

Structures: 

There are two uninhabited historic structures in the area, Johnson’s Lee Equipment Shed on the western edge, and Wreck Line Camp Cabin on the eastern edge. Unfortunately, both have been confirmed destroyed. An additional storage structure was also destroyed adjacent to Johnson’s Lee Equipment Shed, along with a wind shelter at a campsite at Water Canyon Campground.
properties.title: Santa Rosa Island Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Seven Cabins Fire started on May 14, 2026 in the Capitan Mountains. Initial attack resources from the Lincoln National Forest local district responded. Shortly after the initial attack, a Complex Incident Management Team was ordered due to the fire burning in rugged inaccessible terrain of the Wilderness area. The fire is being suppressed using direct extinguishment and indirect confinement strategies. Firefighters are conducting offensive ignitions from indirect control lines in flatter terrain where firelines are more likely to hold and it is safer for responders. Firefighters are working to protect private land and ranches, infrastructure and natural resources.
properties.title: Seven Cabins Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Flanders Fire is located 10 miles east of Breezy Point, Minnesota

The Flanders Fire was reported on Saturday May 16, 2026 and quickly grew from the initial 100 acre size up to over 1,600 acres as of May 20, 2026. An extensive number of aircraft were used during the first shift. MNICS Team B took command of the fire on May 17, 2026 and transferred command of the fire back to the local unit on May 21, 2026.

After more accurate mapping, the fire is estimated at 1712 acres and is 100% contained.
properties.title: Flanders Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Blue Diamond Fire started at approximately 4:00 PM on May 17, 2026, approximately 29 miles northwest of Amarillo, TX and was initially responded to by local fire departments. Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to provide assistance with aircraft, heavy equipment and firefighters. The fire is currently in joint command between Texas A&M Forest Service and local firefighters.

May 17, 8:00 p.m. - Forward progression of the fire is stopped.

Update May 18, 8:00 a.m. - There are no visible smokes on the fire. Firefighters will mop-up and cold trail the right flank of the fire today where dozer line cannot be constructed. Where possible, dozers will be utilized. 

FINAL UPDATE: As of 11:45 p.m. on May 18, 2026, the fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. This is the final update for this fire.
properties.title: Blue Diamond Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Stinky Fire started at approximately 4:24 PM on May 17, 2026, approximately 3.5 miles northeast of Bushland, TX and was initially responded to by local fire departments. Texas A&M Forest Service was requested to provide assistance with aircraft, heavy equipment and firefighters. At 7:00 p.m. that day, Texas A&M Forest Service was released from the fire. On Monday, May 18th at 4:00 p.m., Texas A&M Forest Service was requested back to the fire due to a breach in containment. The fire is currently in joint command between Texas A&M Forest Service and Amarillo Fire Department. Local fire departments on scene of the fire include Idalou, Ralls, Motley County, Floydada, Hale Center, Happy, Hart, Abernathy, Canyon, Buffalo Springs, Roosevelt, Meadow, Denver City, Plainview, Kress, Tuila, Halfway, West Carlisle  and New Deal.

Update May 21, 2026 9:00 a.m. - Firefighters continue to patrol and mop-up. No heat has been found on the fire.

FINAL UPDATE May 21, 2026 6:30 p.m. - The Stinky Fire is 100% contained. All state resources are off of the fire. 

Roads and neighborhoods are now open.  If you have questions, please contact the Amarillo Area Office of Emergency Management.
properties.title: Stinky Fire

properties.field_incident_description: Wildfire across three counties in Southwest Kansas. The counties of Morton, Meade, and Clark.
properties.field_incident_overview:
On May 14, 2026 a powerful storm system moved through southwest Kansas. Lightning strikes, fueled by strong winds and very dry vegetation, started multiple fires across the landscape. The fires quickly surpassed the local and mutual aid response. State assistance was requested. Aviation, KFS DFMOs, and in-state Wildfire Taskforces were mobilized. Many of the initial fires merged and are now being managed as a complex fire. Kansas Governor Kelly issued a State of Emergency Declaration on Friday, May 15, 2026. Local declarations have been issued; there is no federal emergency declaration. 

On Monday, May 18, the Meade County Fires are now under one Incident Command System and will be called the Meade Lake Complex. 

On May 20, 2026, the fire region received precipitation and higher humidity, which helped increase containment on both complexes. Non-local resources will be returned to their home units, and the fires will be monitored and patrolled by the local fire departments. The weather conditions in southwest Kansas have not dramatically changed. There is still potential for new starts to burn intensely and grow rapidly. 

The fires under the Meade Lake Complex are the Wolf Canyon Fire, the Meade County Complex 1 and 2, the Proffitt Lake fire, and the 2 unstaffed fires in the southern part of the county.  

Meade Lake Complex -  Jeff Latham and Troy Mueller - Incident Commanders      

  • 91,616 acres
  • 100% contained

The 500-Acre Pond Fire and the Herman Range Fire grew together on Sunday May, 17, 2026. All fires in Clark County will also be a complex of fires. The complex will include the Bouziden Range Fire and the Lexington Fire

Herman Range Complex - Chad Maybery Incident Commander

On Monday, May 18, 2026, the Herman Ranch Fire made a significant run to the south when the forecasted cold front impacted the fire’s open containment lines along the southern edge of the incident. The acres and map do not reflect that event. 

  • 36,194 acres
  • 90% Contained
properties.title: Southwest Kansas Wildfire Outbreak

properties.field_incident_overview:
New updates will be provided when conditions on the fire change or significant activity occurs.

(May 21, 2026) The Jericho Creek Fire, located approximately 16 miles southwest of Helena, MT remains at approximately 1,000 acres.

The fire area received over one quarter inch of precipitation since yesterday afternoon and more precipitation is expected today.

Firefighters continue to work on indirect confinement strategies due to heavy downed timber and a large concentration of dangerous standing dead trees (“snags”) that pose challenges to ensuring firefighter safety.

Resources assigned to the fire include one hotshot crew, one dozer, two masticators, one excavator, two engines, three suppression modules, and a Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) module.

No closures are in effect, but the public is asked to avoid the Continental Divide area south of MacDonald Pass to Minnehaha/Bryon Creek Road.
properties.title: Jericho Creek

properties.field_incident_overview:
At 4:00 PM on Monday, May 18th, Texas A&M Forest Service received a request for assistance for a fire approximately 13 miles northwest of Plainview, Texas in Swisher County. Heavy equipment, firefighters and aviation resources were requested to assist with the fire. Texas A&M Forest Service crews are working in unified command with local resources.

Update - May 18, 7:00 p.m. Forward progression of the fire has been stopped. Equipment are working to reinforce the right flank of the fire in advance of tonight's forecasted wind shifts. 

The voluntary evacuation that was in place for the City of Kress has been lifted, according to the City of Kress Facebook page. 

FINAL UPDATE: The fire is 100% contained as of 6:15 p.m. on May 19, 2026. All state resources are off of the fire.
properties.title: Kress Fire
